original_hypothesis.title: DoS on All Trading by Wrapping All Tokens to ERC20 — addFees Reverts on Zero totalSupply
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: FeeSplitter.totalSupply() returns (curvesTokenSupply - curvesTokenBalance[token][address(curves)]) * PRECISION, excluding tokens locked in Curves (wrapped to ERC20). If all circulating tokens are withdrawn to ERC20, totalSupply becomes 0, causing addFees to revert with NoTokenHolders. Since _transferFees calls addFees (when holdersFeePercent > 0), ALL buys and sells revert, completely halting trading for that token.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Any token holder can withdraw to ERC20. If enough holders do this (or one whale wraps all but the 1 locked first token), trading halts. The subject always holds the first token, but if even they withdraw, totalSupply can reach 0.

proof_of_signal.pattern: totalSupply excludes tokens held by Curves contract; addFees reverts if totalSupply_ == 0; _transferFees calls addFees when holdersFeePercent > 0

preconditions: All internal token holders withdraw to ERC20
Only the 1 locked first-token remains (held by Curves contract, excluded from totalSupply)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: totalSupply can reach 0 when all tokens are wrapped; addFees reverts on 0 supply
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: addFees revert propagates through _transferFees, blocking all buy/sell operations
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No fallback handling when totalSupply is 0 in _transferFees

analysis_questions: What is the minimum number of tokens that must be wrapped to trigger DoS?
Can the subject's first token be wrapped?
Is there a recovery path once DoS is triggered?
validation_requirements: Wrap all tokens except first, attempt buy/sell, verify revert

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) Subject creates token, buys 1 token. 2) Subject withdraws their 1 token to ERC20. 3) Now curvesTokenBalance[token][curves_contract] = 1, curvesTokenSupply = 1, totalSupply = (1-1)*PRECISION = 0. 4) Any buy/sell of this token reverts because addFees reverts on zero totalSupply.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete trading halt for affected token — users cannot buy or sell
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All traders of the affected token
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Possible if someone deposits ERC20 tokens back, restoring totalSupply > 0

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test for trading behavior when all tokens are wrapped

validation_result.mechanism: addFees revert on zero totalSupply blocks sells
validation_result.impact_summary: When withdrawals to ERC20 cause the FeeSplitter's effective totalSupply to reach 0 after a sell, addFees reverts with NoTokenHolders, permanently blocking certain sell operations and trapping user funds.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis correctly identifies a code defect in the interaction between FeeSplitter.addFees() and the withdraw/sell flow. However, the impact is partially overstated: **buys are NOT blocked** because Curves._buyCurvesToken increments curvesTokenSupply BEFORE calling _transferFees, so the totalSupply seen by addFees is always > 0 for buys. **Certain sells ARE blocked** when the sell would cause (curvesTokenSupply - curvesTokenBalance[token][address(curves)]) to reach 0. This happens when the number of non-locked internal tokens equals the sell amount, leaving only locked (ERC20-wrapped) tokens in the supply. The defect is real: addFees should gracefully handle zero totalSupply (e.g., by skipping fee distribution) rather than reverting.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Three key code locations confirm the defect: (1) FeeSplitter.totalSupply() at lines 34-37 excludes locked tokens, (2) FeeSplitter.addFees() at lines 82-85 reverts on zero totalSupply, (3) Curves.sellCurvesToken() decrements supply BEFORE calling _transferFees, unlike _buyCurvesToken which increments BEFORE. This asymmetry means sells can trigger the zero-supply revert while buys cannot.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: totalSupply can reach 0 when all non-locked internal tokens are sold and only ERC20-wrapped tokens remain. This value is (curvesTokenSupply - lockedInCurves) * PRECISION, which reaches 0 when curvesTokenSupply == lockedInCurves.

Reverts with NoTokenHolders when totalSupply is 0. This revert propagates through _transferFees, blocking the sell transaction entirely. The function should handle zero totalSupply gracefully instead of reverting.

Supply is decremented BEFORE _transferFees is called. If after decrement, (supply - amount) == curvesTokenBalance[token][address(curves)], totalSupply becomes 0, and addFees reverts, blocking the sell.
When holdersFeePercent > 0, addFees is always called. If addFees reverts due to zero totalSupply, the entire sell transaction reverts. No fallback or try-catch mechanism exists.

calling_sequence: 2. Supply is decremented: curvesTokenSupply[subject] = supply - amount (line 239)
5. addFees reads totalSupply(token) which calls curves.curvesTokenSupply and curves.curvesTokenBalance (FeeSplitter line 83)
6. totalSupply = (supply - amount - lockedInCurves) * PRECISION. If supply - amount == lockedInCurves, this is 0
8. Revert propagates back through _transferFees to sellCurvesToken, entire transaction fails
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: Concrete scenario: Supply=3, locked(wrapped)=2, User holds 1 internal token. User calls sellCurvesToken(subject, 1). After decrement: supply=2, locked=2. totalSupply = (2-2)*1e18 = 0. addFees reverts. Sell is blocked. Note: _buyCurvesToken at Curves.sol line 232 increments supply BEFORE calling _transferFees, so buys are NOT affected—after increment, totalSupply will be (old_supply + amount - locked) * PRECISION which is > 0 as long as amount > 0.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided that set fee percentages. The holdersFeePercent defaults to 0 and must be set via setExternalFeePercent. Given the broken Security.sol modifiers (onlyManager is a no-op), anyone can set this value.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Validated scenario: (1) Subject creates token, buys 1 token (supply=1). (2) User A buys 2 tokens (supply=3). (3) Subject withdraws 1 token to ERC20 (locked=1). (4) User A withdraws 1 token to ERC20 (locked=2). (5) User A tries to sell remaining 1 internal token: supply becomes 2, locked=2, totalSupply=0, addFees reverts. User A cannot sell. The hypothesis's claim that buys also revert is INCORRECT—buys increment supply before calling _transferFees, so totalSupply is always > 0 for buys. Sells are the vulnerable path due to supply decrement happening before the fee check.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH severity. While buys continue to work (supply incremented before fee distribution), certain sells are permanently blocked when they would cause totalSupply to reach 0. This means: (1) Users can be trapped with unsellable tokens, (2) Other users' withdraw-to-ERC20 actions can unilaterally create this condition, (3) The user's only recourse is to also withdraw to ERC20 (losing ability to sell through bonding curve). Recovery is possible if someone deposits ERC20 tokens back, but this requires voluntary cooperation.
recommended_mitigations: In _transferFees, wrap the addFees call in a conditional: if totalSupply > 0, call addFees; otherwise, redirect the holderFee to protocol or refund to sender
Alternatively, modify addFees to return early (without reverting) when totalSupply is 0, potentially sending the ETH to a fallback destination
Consider using try/catch around feeRedistributor.addFees to prevent fee distribution failures from blocking core trading operations
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy all contracts with holdersFeePercent > 0. 2. Subject buys 1 token. 3. User A buys 2 tokens. 4. Subject withdraws 1 token to ERC20. 5. User A withdraws 1 token to ERC20. 6. User A attempts sellCurvesToken(subject, 1) — expect revert with NoTokenHolders. 7. Verify a new buy still works (buyCurvesToken succeeds). 8. Verify User A depositing ERC20 back allows selling again.
uncertainties: The hypothesis overstates impact by claiming ALL buys and sells revert. Buys are NOT affected due to supply being incremented before _transferFees. Only specific sells that would reduce totalSupply to 0 are blocked.
Whether holdersFeePercent is ever set to > 0 in production deployment is unverifiable from available scripts, though the broken access control means anyone could set it.
additional_testing_needed: Test the exact boundary: sell that leaves totalSupply = 1 (should work) vs totalSupply = 0 (should revert)
Test sellExternalCurvesToken path to verify it also fails (deposit then sell would still hit the same issue)
Test recovery by depositing ERC20 tokens back to restore non-zero totalSupply
